sections.label: Paragraph 1
sections.original_text: Appendix [X] — Proposed Service Commitment Framework
sections.revised_text: Appendix [X] — Proposed Service Commitment Framework

sections.label: Paragraph 3
sections.original_text: Power Wood Canada Corp. v Canadian National Railway Company
sections.revised_text: Power Wood Canada Corp. v Canadian National Railway Company

sections.label: Paragraph 4
sections.original_text: CTA Case No. 26-05910
sections.revised_text: CTA Case No. 26-05910

sections.label: Paragraph 7
sections.original_text: 1.	Status and Legal Character
sections.revised_text: 1.	Status and Legal Character

sections.label: Paragraph 9
sections.original_text: 1.1	This Proposed Service Commitment Framework (the “Framework”) is filed by Power Wood Canada Corp. (“Power Wood”) as Appendix [X] to its Reply in CTA Case No. 26-05910. It constitutes a formal undertaking by Power Wood with respect to the Traffic within the meaning of s. 116(1.2)(d) of the Canada Transportation Act, SC 1996, c 10 (the “CTA”), and is offered in support of, and as consistent with, a level-of-service determination under ss. 113–116 CTA and, in the alternative, a shipper undertaking under s. 169.31 CTA.
sections.revised_text: 1.1	This Proposed Service Commitment Framework (the “Framework”) is filed by Power Wood Canada Corp. (“Power Wood”) as Appendix [X] to its Reply in CTA Case No. 26-05910. It constitutes a formal undertaking by Power Wood with respect to the Traffic (within the meaning of s. 116(1.2)(d) of the Canada Transportation Act, SC 1996, c 10 (the “CTA”)), and is offered in support of, and as consistent with, a level-of-service determination under ss. 113–116 CTA and, in the alternative, a shipper undertaking under s. 169.31 CTA.

sections.label: Paragraph 11
sections.original_text: 1.2	The operative clauses that follow are drafted so that they may be reflected in an Agency order directing Canadian National Railway Company (“CN”) to provide service on terms no less favourable than those set out herein, as contemplated in the relief sought in the Reply.
sections.revised_text: 1.2	The operative clauses that follow are drafted so that they may be reflected in an Agency order directing Canadian National Railway Company (“CN”) to provide service on terms no less favourable than those set out herein, as contemplated in the relief sought in the Reply.

sections.label: Paragraph 13
sections.original_text: 1.3	CN’s Answer states, at paragraph 24, that “at no point has CN refused to service Power Wood” and that CN has “proposed alternatives to mitigate” capacity constraints. On that pleading, the question before the Agency is not whether a service obligation exists but whether the conditions CN has attached to performance — including the Pickardville capital contribution referred to below — are commercially fair and reasonable within the meaning of s. 112 CTA. The Framework is drafted to allow that question to be answered on a concrete and orderable record.
sections.revised_text: 1.3	CN’s Answer states, at paragraph 24, that “at no point has CN refused to service Power Wood” and that CN has “proposed alternatives to mitigate” capacity constraints. On that pleading, the question before the Agency is not whether a service obligation exists but whether the conditions CN has attached to performance - including the Pickardville Demand defined below - are commercially fair and reasonable within the meaning of s. 112 CTA. This Framework is drafted to allow that question to be answered on a concrete and orderable record.

sections.label: Paragraph 16
sections.original_text: 2.	Background and Basis
sections.revised_text: 2.	Background and Basis

sections.label: Paragraph 18
sections.original_text: 2.1	The project. Power Wood is developing a black pellet manufacturing facility near La Crête, Alberta. The Traffic (black pellets, bulk density approximately 750 kg/m³) moves by truck from La Crête to a dedicated transload at High Level, Alberta, and from High Level by covered hopper unit train on CN’s Mackenzie Northern Railway (the “MKNR”) to the Port of Prince Rupert for export to Japanese electricity generators.
sections.revised_text: 2.1	The Project. Power Wood is developing a black pellet manufacturing facility near La Crête, Alberta. The Traffic (black pellets, bulk density approximately 750 kg/m³) moves by truck from La Crête to a dedicated transload at High Level, Alberta, and from High Level by covered hopper unit train on CN’s Mackenzie Northern Railway (the “MKNR”) to the Port of Prince Rupert for export to Japanese electricity generators.

sections.label: Paragraph 20
sections.original_text: 2.2	Circularity. Power Wood cannot close its construction financing without a CN service commitment on lender-bankable terms. CN has to date declined to commit because Power Wood has not yet tendered actual traffic. Power Wood cannot tender actual traffic because, without a service commitment, it cannot close financing and build. The Framework resolves that mutual dependence by synchronizing both parties’ obligations on the parallel conditions precedent in paragraph 8.
sections.revised_text: 2.2	Circularity. Power Wood cannot close its construction financing without a CN service commitment on lender-bankable terms. CN has to date declined to commit because it alleges Power Wood has not yet tendered actual traffic. Power Wood has given estimates, but cannot tender actual traffic, because, without a service commitment, it cannot close financing and build. This Framework resolves that mutual dependence by synchronizing both parties’ obligations on the parallel conditions precedent in paragraph 8.

sections.label: Paragraph 22
sections.original_text: 2.3	2019–2024 engagement. Between 2019 and 2024, CN actively participated in shaping the commercial and operational parameters now reflected in this Framework. Without limitation: (a) CN recommended Mr. Greg Karpo (a 37-year CN operations veteran, now an independent rail consultant) to Power Wood as the expert to design the unit train transload concept at High Level; (b) CN recommended shifting the export routing from the Port of Vancouver to the Port of Prince Rupert, which Power Wood adopted on CN’s advice; (c) CN contributed to the design of the High Level unit train loop and issued the design parameters against which Power Wood’s approximately $11 million track and transload capital commitment was structured; (d) on March 18, 2024, CN advised that it could accommodate an initial service level of approximately 20 covered hopper cars per week on the MKNR; and (e) on or about April 17, 2024, CN declined to provide the service commitment requested and conditioned further engagement on a capital contribution by Power Wood (estimated at approximately $53 million) toward a proposed siding at Pickardville, Alberta (the “Pickardville Demand”).
sections.revised_text: 2.3	2019–2024 engagement. Between 2019 and 2024, CN actively participated in shaping the commercial and operational parameters now reflected in this Framework. Without limitation: (a) CN recommended Mr. Greg Karpo (a 37-year CN operations veteran, now an independent rail consultant) to Power Wood as the expert to design the unit train transload concept at High Level; (b) CN recommended shifting the export routing from the Port of Vancouver to the Port of Prince Rupert, which Power Wood adopted on CN’s advice; (c) CN contributed to the design of the High Level unit train loop and issued the design parameters against which Power Wood’s approximately $11 million track and transload capital commitment was structured; (d) on March 18, 2024, CN advised that it could accommodate an initial service level of approximately 20 covered hopper cars per week on the MKNR; and (e) on or about April 17, 2024, CN declined to provide the service commitment requested and conditioned further engagement on a capital contribution by Power Wood (estimated at approximately $53 million) toward an upgrade of rail infrastructure at various locations including, but not limited to, McLennan Yard and Pickardville (the “Pickardville Demand”).

sections.label: Paragraph 24
sections.original_text: 2.4	CN prior positions incorporated. The following positions taken by CN during the 2019–2024 engagement are incorporated into this Framework as the operational spine of the arrangement: (a) a Phase 1 operational level of approximately 20 cars per week (CN’s March 18, 2024 position); (b) the Port of Prince Rupert as destination (CN’s routing recommendation); (c) the design parameters of the High Level unit train loop and transload (CN’s engineering input); and (d) the covered hopper car configuration and Manning Subdivision operating assumptions (CN’s operational input). None of the foregoing is a Power Wood demand on CN.
sections.revised_text: 2.4	CN prior positions incorporated. The following positions taken by CN during the 2019–2024 engagement are incorporated into this Framework as the operational spine of the arrangement: (a) a Phase 1 operational level of approximately 20 cars per week (CN’s March 18, 2024 position); (b) the Port of Prince Rupert as destination (CN’s routing recommendation); (c) the design parameters of the High Level unit train loop and transload (CN’s engineering input); and (d) the covered hopper car configuration and Manning Subdivision operating assumptions (CN’s operational input). None of the foregoing is a Power Wood demand on CN.

sections.label: Paragraph 26
sections.original_text: 2.5	Karpo Report findings. The Rail Expert Opinion Report of Mr. Greg Karpo dated December 29, 2025 (the “Karpo Report”), filed as Appendix A to the Application, establishes that the MKNR has sufficient existing operational capacity to accommodate Power Wood’s Phase 2 full-capacity service (approximately 36 unit trains per year, an approximately 3.8% increase in corridor traffic) with only incremental crew and locomotive positioning and without any new siding or other major capital upgrade. MKNR corridor volumes have declined since 2021, and the Phase 2 volume increase sits within CN’s own stated corridor growth envelope.
sections.revised_text: 2.5	Karpo Report findings. The Rail Expert Opinion Report of Mr. Greg Karpo dated December 29, 2025 (the “Karpo Report”), filed as Appendix A to the Application, establishes that the MKNR has sufficient existing operational capacity to accommodate Power Wood’s Phase 2 full-capacity service (approximately 36 unit trains per year, an approximately 3.8% increase in corridor traffic relative to 2024 traffic) with only incremental crew and locomotive positioning and without any new siding or other major capital upgrade. MKNR corridor volumes have declined since 2021, and the Phase 2 volume increase sits within CN’s own stated corridor growth envelope.

sections.label: Paragraph 28
sections.original_text: 2.6	Power Wood’s sunk commitments. Power Wood has incurred approximately $35 million in pre-construction expenditures (land, permits, front-end engineering and design, and professional fees), is approximately 70–80% through front-end engineering and design with Valmet, SNC Lavalin/AtkinsRealis, and WB Melback, holds off-take arrangements with Sumitomo Corporation and additional Japanese counterparties covering aggregate committed volumes at or above Phase 2 full capacity, and has construction financing committed in principle at approximately $150 million subject to conditions precedent that include a CN service commitment on lender-bankable terms.
sections.revised_text: 2.6	Power Wood’s sunk commitments. Power Wood has incurred approximately $35 million in pre-construction expenditures (land, permits, front-end engineering and design, and professional fees), is approximately 70–80% through front-end engineering and design with Valmet, SNC Lavalin/AtkinsRealis, and WB Melback, holds letters of intent for off-take arrangements with Sumitomo Corporation and additional Japanese counterparties covering aggregate committed volumes at or above Phase 2 full capacity, and has construction financing at approximately $150 million under letters of intent, subject to conditions precedent that include a CN service commitment on lender-bankable terms.

sections.label: Paragraph 30
sections.original_text: [NTD: Client to confirm which off-take instruments are binding executed agreements and which remain committed letters of intent, and to provide copies for exhibit review. CN’s Answer at paragraph 49 characterizes all such instruments as non-binding LOIs; that characterization is contested and is to be corrected on the record where factually incorrect. A lender letter confirming that the CN service commitment is a specific condition precedent to Financial Close is to be filed as an exhibit, forthwith if not appended to the Reply.]
sections.revised_text: [NTD: Client to confirm which off-take instruments are binding executed agreements and which remain committed letters of intent, and to provide copies for exhibit review. CN’s Answer at paragraph 49 characterizes all such instruments as non-binding LOIs; that characterization is contested and is to be corrected on the record where factually incorrect. A lender letter confirming that the CN service commitment is a specific condition precedent to Financial Close is to be filed as an exhibit, forthwith if not appended to the Reply.]

sections.label: Paragraph 33
sections.original_text: 3.	CN Service Commitment
sections.revised_text: 3.	CN Service Commitment

sections.label: Paragraph 35
sections.original_text: 3.1	Core commitment. CN will, during the Service Period, furnish adequate and suitable accommodation for the receiving, loading, carriage, and delivery of the Traffic in covered hopper cars of minimum 4,850 cubic feet capacity from the High Level transload to the Port of Prince Rupert, in accordance with ss. 113–114 CTA, the Volume Schedule in paragraph 3.2, and the Service Reliability KPIs in paragraph 3.4.
sections.revised_text: 3.1	Core commitment. CN will, during the Service Period, furnish adequate and suitable accommodation for the receiving, loading, carriage, and delivery of the Traffic in covered hopper cars of minimum 4,850 cubic feet capacity from the High Level transload to the Port of Prince Rupert, in accordance with ss. 113–114 CTA, the Volume Schedule in paragraph 3.2, and the Service Reliability KPIs in paragraph 3.4.

sections.label: Paragraph 37
sections.original_text: 3.2	Volume Schedule. Commencing on the Commencement Date, CN will accommodate the Traffic on the following phased basis:
sections.revised_text: 3.2	Volume Schedule. Commencing on the Commencement Date, CN will accommodate the Traffic on the following phased basis:

sections.label: Paragraph 39
sections.original_text: (a)	Commissioning Period (Months 1–3): approximately 10 cars per week, with flexible scheduling matched to Power Wood’s actual production output; KPI measurement for baseline purposes only.
sections.revised_text: (a)	Commissioning Period (Months 1–3): approximately 10 cars per week, operated as partial train sets by accommodation of CN, with flexible scheduling matched to Power Wood’s actual production output; KPI measurement for baseline purposes only.

sections.label: Paragraph 41
sections.original_text: (b)	Phase 1 (Months 4–24): commencing at approximately 20 cars per week (reflecting CN’s March 18, 2024 position) and ramping to approximately 40 cars per week by the end of Month 24.
sections.revised_text: (b)	Phase 1 (Months 4–24): commencing at approximately one Unit Train per month and ramping to approximately one Unit Train every 10 days by the end of Month 24 (each Unit Train consisting of approximately 105 covered hopper cars).

sections.label: Paragraph 43
sections.original_text: (c)	Phase 2 (Month 25 onwards): approximately 77 cars per week, approximately 3,813 carloads per year, and approximately 36 Unit Trains per year (approximately 105 cars per Unit Train).
sections.revised_text: (c)	Phase 2 (Month 25 onwards): approximately 36 Unit Trains per year (approximately one every 10 days), approximately 3,813 carloads per year at approximately 105 cars per Unit Train.

sections.label: Paragraph 44
sections.revised_text: For the avoidance of doubt, following the Commissioning Period the Volume Schedule is expressed and measured in Unit Trains; service level adjustments shall be made by changing Unit Train cadence (for example, from one Unit Train per month to one Unit Train every 10 days) and not by tendering partial train sets.

sections.label: Paragraph 46
sections.original_text: [NTD: Confirm whether the Phase 1 to Phase 2 transition is triggered on a time basis, on demonstrated sustained production capability, or both.]
sections.revised_text: [NTD: Confirm whether the Phase 1 to Phase 2 transition is triggered on a time basis, on demonstrated sustained production capability, or both.]

sections.label: Paragraph 48
sections.original_text: 3.3	Car supply. Primary Position: CN will supply the covered hopper cars required for the Traffic as part of its service obligation, with the ordinary capital and maintenance cost of the car pool borne by CN and reflected in the Freight Rate. CN will maintain a dedicated car pool sufficient to meet the Volume Schedule at each operational phase. Alternative Position: if the parties agree that a private car pool structure is commercially preferable, Power Wood will lease or acquire a dedicated fleet of covered hopper cars, tender those cars to CN for movement, and pay a reduced Freight Rate. The Primary Position is preferred as consistent with CN’s s. 113 accommodation duty and as avoiding approximately $40–60 million of additional capital cost on Power Wood.
sections.revised_text: 3.3	Car supply. CN will supply the covered hopper cars required for the Traffic as part of its service obligation, with the ordinary capital and maintenance cost of the car pool borne by CN and reflected in the Freight Rate. CN will maintain a dedicated car pool sufficient to meet the Volume Schedule at each operational phase.

sections.label: Paragraph 50
sections.original_text: 3.4	Service Reliability KPIs. CN’s performance will be measured on a rolling quarterly basis (commencing at the end of the Commissioning Period) against the following reciprocal standards:
sections.revised_text: 3.4	Service Reliability KPIs. CN’s performance will be measured on a rolling quarterly basis (commencing at the end of the Commissioning Period) against the following reciprocal standards:

sections.label: Paragraph 52
sections.original_text: (a)	Car placement: not less than 95% of ordered cars placed at High Level within the loading window specified in Power Wood’s 30-day confirmed loading notice;
sections.revised_text: (a)	Car placement: not less than 95% of ordered cars placed at High Level within the loading window specified in Power Wood’s 30-day confirmed loading notice;

sections.label: Paragraph 54
sections.original_text: (b)	Transit time: average loaded transit time from High Level to Prince Rupert within a benchmark to be set in the definitive agreement [NTD: confirm with Greg Karpo — proposed 96 hours for Phase 2 Unit Trains];
sections.revised_text: (b)	Transit time: average loaded transit time from High Level to Prince Rupert of approximately 9 days per Unit Train [NTD: confirm tolerance band with Greg Karpo];

sections.label: Paragraph 56
sections.original_text: (c)	Empty car return / origin dwell: average empty car return and dwell at High Level not exceeding 48 hours from Power Wood’s notice of need; and
sections.revised_text: (c)	Empty car return / origin dwell: average dwell of empty Unit Train consists at High Level of between 72 and 96 hours from placement, reflecting a transload throughput of approximately 20 minutes per railcar and approximately 36 cars per 12-hour shift plus inspections and ancillary operations [NTD: confirm with Greg Karpo based on final transload design]; and

sections.label: Paragraph 58
sections.original_text: (d)	Train origination: not less than 95% of scheduled Unit Train originations on the confirmed loading date.
sections.revised_text: (d)	Train origination: not less than 95% of scheduled Unit Train originations on the confirmed loading date.

sections.label: Paragraph 60
sections.original_text: Consequences of failure. If CN fails to meet any KPI in a measurement period, Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation is reduced proportionally by the volumes affected. If performance on a metric falls below 90% of target for two consecutive quarters, Power Wood is entitled to a Freight Rate credit equal to 10% of the variable cost component of the affected movements. Performance below 85% of target for four consecutive quarters constitutes a material breach entitling Power Wood to terminate this Framework without penalty and to resume all statutory CTA remedies.
sections.revised_text: Consequences of failure. If CN fails to meet any KPI in a measurement period, Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation is reduced proportionally by the volumes affected. If performance on a metric falls below 90% of target for two consecutive quarters, Power Wood is entitled to a Freight Rate credit equal to 10% of the variable cost component of the affected movements. Performance below 85% of target for four consecutive quarters constitutes a material breach entitling Power Wood to terminate this Framework without penalty and to pursue all damages and all remedies available at law or in equity, including (without limitation) all civil remedies and statutory remedies under the CTA.

sections.label: Paragraph 62
sections.original_text: 3.5	Advance notice of operational constraints. CN will provide not less than 90 days’ prior written notice of any planned maintenance, capacity constraint, or other circumstance reasonably anticipated to affect its ability to meet its obligations, together with the nature and anticipated duration of the constraint, the portion of the Volume Schedule or KPIs affected, and CN’s proposed mitigation plan. During any such notice period, Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation is suspended to the extent of the affected volumes.
sections.revised_text: 3.5 Advance notice of operational constraints. CN will provide not less than 90 days’ prior written notice of any planned maintenance, capacity constraint, or other circumstance reasonably anticipated to affect its ability to meet its obligations (a “Constraint Period”), together with the nature and anticipated duration of the constraint, the portion of the Volume Schedule or KPIs affected, and CN’s proposed mitigation plan. If the anticipated operational constraint is expected to last more than 7 days, CN will provide not less than 120 days’ prior written notice. During any such Constraint Period, Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation is suspended to the extent of the affected volumes.

sections.label: Paragraph 66
sections.original_text: 4.	Power Wood Volume, Take-or-Pay, and Security Commitments
sections.revised_text: 4.	Power Wood Volume, Take-or-Pay, and Security Commitments

sections.label: Paragraph 68
sections.original_text: 4.1	Take-or-pay obligation. Power Wood will tender, or pay CN for the equivalent of, the following minimum annual carload volumes during each year of the Service Period: (a) during the Commissioning Period, no minimum applies; (b) during Phase 1, 80% of the applicable ramping Volume Schedule; and (c) during Phase 2, 80% of the Phase 2 Volume Schedule (approximately 3,050 carloads per year).
sections.revised_text: 4.1	Take-or-pay obligation. Power Wood will tender, or pay CN for the equivalent of, the following minimum annual carload volumes during each year of the Service Period: (a) during the Commissioning Period, no minimum applies; (b) during Phase 1, 80% of the applicable ramping Volume Schedule; and (c) during Phase 2, 80% of the Phase 2 Volume Schedule (approximately 3,050 carloads per year).

sections.label: Paragraph 70
sections.original_text: 4.2	Two-tier structure. For any shortfall between the take-or-pay floor and carloads actually tendered, Power Wood will pay CN a reservation fee equal to [NTD: confirm — proposed 50%] of the variable cost component of the Freight Rate applied to the shortfall carloads, reflecting CN’s reserved-capacity cost on cars not tendered. For carloads actually tendered and moved, Power Wood will pay the full Freight Rate. Shortfall is calculated quarterly with a year-end true-up.
sections.revised_text: 4.2	Two-tier structure. For any shortfall between the take-or-pay floor and carloads actually tendered, Power Wood will pay CN a reservation fee equal to [NTD: confirm — proposed 50%] of the variable cost component of the Freight Rate applied to the shortfall carloads, reflecting CN’s reserved-capacity cost on cars not tendered. For carloads actually tendered and moved, Power Wood will pay the full Freight Rate. Shortfall is calculated quarterly with a year-end true-up.

sections.label: Paragraph 72
sections.original_text: 4.3	Suspension events. The take-or-pay obligation is suspended during: (a) any failure by CN to meet a Service Reliability KPI, to the extent of the affected volumes; (b) any period covered by a CN advance notice of operational constraint; (c) Force Majeure; (d) any period in which Power Wood is unable to tender Traffic by reason of an insured plant disruption, with the insurance proceeds satisfying the obligation to the extent of the insured period; and (e) any material default by CN.
sections.revised_text: 4.3	Suspension events. The take-or-pay obligation is suspended during: (a) any failure by CN to meet a Service Reliability KPI, to the extent of the affected volumes; (b) any period covered by a CN advance notice of operational constraint; (c) Force Majeure; (d) any period in which Power Wood is unable to tender Traffic by reason of an insured plant disruption, with the insurance proceeds satisfying the obligation to the extent of the insured period; and (e) any material default by CN.

sections.label: Paragraph 74
sections.original_text: 4.4	Notice obligations. Power Wood will provide CN with not less than (a) 24 months’ notice of Financial Close and the construction completion schedule; (b) 12 months’ notice of the Phase 2 ramp-up, with a preliminary monthly volume forecast; (c) 60 days’ rolling monthly volume requirements before each calendar month; and (d) 30 days’ confirmed loading date and car order before each Unit Train.
sections.revised_text: 4.4	Notice obligations. Power Wood will provide CN with not less than (a) 24 months’ notice of Financial Close [NTD: confirm 24-month period with the business]; (b) 12 months’ notice of the Phase 2 ramp-up, with a preliminary monthly volume forecast; (c) 60 days’ rolling monthly volume requirements before each calendar month; and (d) 30 days’ confirmed loading date and car order before each Unit Train.

sections.label: Paragraph 76
sections.original_text: 4.5	Letter of credit security. Power Wood will maintain, in favour of CN from the Commencement Date throughout the Service Period, a renewable standby letter of credit (the “L/C”) issued by a Canadian Schedule I chartered bank (or another issuer rated not less than A‑/A3 acceptable to CN), in an amount equal to three months of the Phase 2 take-or-pay reservation fee exposure (estimated at approximately $5.1 million and scaling down for the Commissioning Period and Phase 1). The L/C renews automatically for successive twelve-month periods unless Power Wood delivers not less than 60 days’ prior written notice of non-renewal (any such non-renewal being an event of default if take-or-pay obligations remain outstanding). CN may draw on the L/C to satisfy any confirmed quarterly shortfall Power Wood has failed to pay within 30 days of invoice, on 10 business days’ prior written notice of intent to draw during which Power Wood may dispute the draw under paragraph 10.
sections.revised_text: 4.5	Letter of credit security. Power Wood will maintain, in favour of CN from the Commencement Date throughout the Service Period, a renewable standby letter of credit (the “L/C”) issued by a Canadian Schedule I chartered bank (or another issuer rated not less than A‑/A3 acceptable to CN), in an amount equal to three months of the Phase 2 take-or-pay reservation fee exposure (estimated at approximately $5.1 million and scaling down for the Commissioning Period and Phase 1). The L/C renews automatically for successive twelve-month periods unless Power Wood delivers not less than 60 days’ prior written notice of non-renewal (any such non-renewal being an event of default if take-or-pay obligations remain outstanding). CN may draw on the L/C to satisfy any confirmed quarterly shortfall Power Wood has failed to pay within 30 days of invoice, on 10 business days’ prior written notice of intent to draw during which Power Wood may dispute the draw under paragraph 10.

sections.label: Paragraph 78
sections.original_text: [NTD: Confirm the exact L/C amount once the reservation fee percentage is fixed, and confirm whether the L/C is lodged from Financial Close or from the Commencement Date.]
sections.revised_text: [NTD: Confirm the exact L/C amount once the reservation fee percentage is fixed, and confirm whether the L/C is lodged from Financial Close or from the Commencement Date.]

sections.label: Paragraph 80
sections.original_text: 4.6	Power Wood infrastructure commitment. Power Wood will construct, at its cost and from its construction financing, the unit train loop, transload, and loading infrastructure at High Level, at an estimated cost of approximately $11 million, in accordance with the design parameters reflected in the CN Prior Positions. The infrastructure will be completed prior to the Commencement Date.
sections.revised_text: 4.6	Power Wood infrastructure commitment. Power Wood will construct, at its cost and from its construction financing, the unit train loop, transload, and loading infrastructure at High Level, at an estimated cost of approximately $11 million, in accordance with the design parameters reflected in the CN Prior Positions. The infrastructure will be completed prior to the Commencement Date. The High Level unit train loop, transload, and loading infrastructure are for Power Wood’s exclusive use, and CN shall not use, permit the use of, or allocate capacity on that infrastructure to any third party without Power Wood’s prior written consent.

sections.label: Paragraph 83
sections.original_text: 5.	Capital Expenditure Allocation
sections.revised_text: 5.	Capital Expenditure Allocation

sections.label: Paragraph 85
sections.original_text: 5.1	Principles. Capital expenditure in connection with the service under this Framework is allocated on the following principles, each an element of commercial reasonableness under s. 112 CTA: (a) Power Wood bears the cost of infrastructure specific to its own operations (the La Crête facility, the High Level unit train loop, and the High Level transload); (b) CN bears the cost of corridor infrastructure, recoverable through standard rates; (c) no capital expenditure is required of either party unless operationally necessary for the service level then in effect; and (d) where any CN expenditure both serves the Traffic and benefits other MKNR users, Power Wood’s contribution is limited to its documented pro-rata share of the incremental benefit Power Wood receives.
sections.revised_text: 5.1	Principles. Capital expenditure in connection with the service under this Framework is allocated on the following principles, each an element of commercial reasonableness under s. 112 CTA: (a) Power Wood bears the cost of infrastructure specific to its own operations (the La Crête facility, the High Level unit train loop, and the High Level transload); (b) CN bears the cost of corridor infrastructure, recoverable through standard rates; (c) no capital expenditure is required of either party unless operationally necessary for the service level then in effect; and (d) where any CN expenditure both serves the Traffic and benefits other MKNR users, Power Wood’s contribution is limited to its documented pro-rata share of the incremental benefit Power Wood receives.

sections.label: Paragraph 87
sections.original_text: 5.2	Phase 1 — no CN capital contribution required. On the findings of the Karpo Report, and on CN’s own March 18, 2024 position that it could accommodate approximately 20 cars per week within existing infrastructure, no CN capital expenditure is operationally required for the Commissioning Period or Phase 1. Power Wood does not accept, and will not contribute to, any CN capital expenditure as a condition of Commissioning Period or Phase 1 service.
sections.revised_text: 5.2	Phase 1 — no CN capital contribution required. On the findings of the Karpo Report, and on CN’s own March 18, 2024 position that it could accommodate approximately 20 cars per week within existing infrastructure, no CN capital expenditure is operationally required for the Commissioning Period or Phase 1. Power Wood does not accept, and will not contribute to, any CN capital expenditure as a condition of Commissioning Period or Phase 1 service.

sections.label: Paragraph 89
sections.original_text: 5.3	Phase 2 — contingent and contested. Primary Position: on the Karpo Report findings, no CN capital expenditure is operationally required for Phase 2 either. A 3.8% corridor traffic increase does not trigger a capacity constraint requiring new infrastructure and sits within CN’s stated growth envelope on a corridor where volumes have declined since 2021. Alternative Position: if, through the dispute process in paragraph 10, the parties agree or an arbitral body determines that specific CN capital expenditure is operationally necessary for Phase 2, Power Wood will contribute only on the basis that (i) the item is specifically and uniquely required for the Traffic and is not part of CN’s ordinary network investment program, (ii) Power Wood’s contribution is limited to its documented pro-rata share of incremental benefit, (iii) CN demonstrates the expenditure is not recoverable through ordinary tariff mechanisms or contributions from other beneficiaries, and (iv) any contribution is subject to the recoupment in paragraph 5.5.
sections.revised_text: 5.3	Phase 2 — contingent and contested. Primary Position: on the Karpo Report findings, no CN capital expenditure is operationally required for Phase 2 either. A 3.8% corridor traffic increase does not trigger a capacity constraint requiring new infrastructure and sits within CN’s stated growth envelope on a corridor where volumes have declined since 2021. Alternative Position: if, through the dispute process in paragraph 10, the parties agree or an arbitral body determines that specific CN capital expenditure is operationally necessary for Phase 2, Power Wood will contribute only on the basis that (i) the item is specifically and uniquely required for the Traffic and is not part of CN’s ordinary network investment program, (ii) Power Wood’s contribution is limited to its documented pro-rata share of incremental benefit, (iii) CN demonstrates the expenditure is not recoverable through ordinary tariff mechanisms or contributions from other beneficiaries, and (iv) any contribution is subject to the recoupment in paragraph 5.5.

sections.label: Paragraph 91
sections.original_text: 5.4	Pickardville Demand — rejected as a blanket precondition. Power Wood does not accept the Pickardville Demand as a precondition to service. The proposed siding is located approximately 460 miles south of High Level, would benefit all existing MKNR customers rather than Power Wood specifically, is not operationally required on the Karpo Report findings, and, on the revenues and margins implied by the Freight Rate applied to Phase 2 volumes, would generate a return to CN exceeding CN’s publicly stated corporate return-on-invested-capital target. The Pickardville Demand therefore reflects a business decision, not a genuine operational or financial obstacle to service.
sections.revised_text: 5.4	Pickardville Demand — rejected as a blanket precondition. Power Wood does not accept the Pickardville Demand as a precondition to service. The proposed siding is located approximately 460 miles south of High Level, would benefit all existing MKNR customers rather than Power Wood specifically, is not operationally required on the Karpo Report findings, and, on the revenues and margins implied by the Freight Rate applied to Phase 2 volumes, would generate a return to CN exceeding CN’s publicly stated corporate return-on-invested-capital target. The Pickardville Demand therefore reflects a business decision, not a genuine operational or financial obstacle to service.

sections.label: Paragraph 93
sections.original_text: 5.5	Third-party benefit recoupment. To the extent any CN capital expenditure is ultimately contributed to by Power Wood under paragraph 5.3 and generates operational benefits for other MKNR users, Power Wood is entitled, at CN’s election, to recoupment on a pro-rata basis by (a) a rate rebate applied to Freight Rate invoices over a defined payback period, (b) a direct cash offset against Power Wood’s contribution to the extent CN recovers cost from other users, or (c) a first-mover benefit credit recognizing the capacity Power Wood’s contribution creates.
sections.revised_text: 5.5	Third-party benefit recoupment. To the extent any CN capital expenditure is ultimately contributed to by Power Wood under paragraph 5.3 and generates operational benefits for other MKNR users, Power Wood is entitled, at Power Wood’s election, to recoupment on a pro-rata basis by (a) a rate rebate applied to Freight Rate invoices over a defined payback period, (b) a direct cash offset against Power Wood’s contribution to the extent CN recovers cost from other users, or (c) a first-mover benefit credit recognizing the capacity Power Wood’s contribution creates.

sections.label: Paragraph 96
sections.original_text: 6.	Rate and Commercial Terms
sections.revised_text: 6.	Rate and Commercial Terms

sections.label: Paragraph 98
sections.original_text: 6.1	Freight Rate. The base Freight Rate is [NTD: confirm — indicative rate of approximately $5,664 per covered hopper carload at Phase 2 volumes, referenced in the Karpo Report as an engagement-period estimate and to be confirmed against CN’s current tariff for comparable long-term unit train movements]. At full Phase 2 capacity, the base rate generates approximately $21.5 million in gross annual revenue to CN.
sections.revised_text: 6.1	Freight Rate. The base Freight Rate is to be confirmed. An indicative figure of approximately $5,664 per covered hopper carload was discussed with CN during the initial 2024 engagement and is referenced in the Karpo Report as an engagement-period estimate only; it has not been confirmed by CN and is subject to confirmation against CN’s current tariff for comparable long-term Unit Train movements. [NTD: confirm CN contact’s name and title and whether the discussion is documented in writing.] At full Phase 2 capacity, the indicative rate would generate on the order of $21.5 million in gross annual revenue to CN, for illustrative purposes only pending rate confirmation.

sections.label: Paragraph 100
sections.original_text: 6.2	Escalation. The Freight Rate is subject to annual escalation, with (a) 70% of the rate indexed to the Canadian Consumer Price Index (or a comparable published cost index to be agreed), capped at 4% per annum, (b) 20% comprising a fuel surcharge component linked to a published diesel fuel price index, and (c) 10% fixed for the duration of the Term. The parties will conduct a market rate review every five years, with any unresolved adjustment referred to dispute resolution under paragraph 10.
sections.revised_text: 6.2	Escalation. The Freight Rate is subject to annual escalation, with (a) 70% of the rate indexed to the Canadian Consumer Price Index (or a comparable published cost index to be agreed), capped at 4% per annum, (b) 20% comprising a fuel surcharge component linked to a published diesel fuel price index, and (c) 10% fixed for the duration of the Term. The parties will conduct a market rate review every five years, with any unresolved adjustment referred to dispute resolution under paragraph 10.

sections.label: Paragraph 103
sections.original_text: 7.	Risk Allocation
sections.revised_text: 7.	Risk Allocation

sections.label: Paragraph 105
sections.original_text: 7.1	Plant disruption insurance — CN as loss payee. Power Wood will maintain, from the Commencement Date throughout the Service Period, business interruption and plant disruption insurance covering the La Crête facility, with CN named as loss payee and additional insured to the extent of CN’s take-or-pay entitlement during any insured disruption period. For any period during which Power Wood is unable to tender Traffic by reason of an insured event, the insurer will pay CN directly in satisfaction of Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation for that period. Where commercially feasible, the CN loss payee coverage will be incorporated into the business interruption coverage Power Wood’s lenders will in any event require as a condition precedent to Financial Close.
sections.revised_text: 7.1	Plant disruption insurance — CN as loss payee. Power Wood will maintain, from the Commencement Date throughout the Service Period, business interruption and plant disruption insurance covering the La Crête facility, with CN named as loss payee and additional insured to the extent of CN’s take-or-pay entitlement during any insured disruption period. For any period during which Power Wood is unable to tender Traffic by reason of an insured event, the insurer will pay CN directly in satisfaction of Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation for that period. Where commercially feasible, the CN loss payee coverage will be incorporated into the business interruption coverage Power Wood’s lenders will in any event require as a condition precedent to Financial Close.

sections.label: Paragraph 107
sections.original_text: [NTD: Insurance counsel to confirm availability, structure, and premium cost of the coverage, and whether it can be delivered through the lender-required policy as a single integrated instrument.]
sections.revised_text: [NTD: Insurance counsel to confirm availability, structure, and premium cost of the coverage, and whether it can be delivered through the lender-required policy as a single integrated instrument.]

sections.label: Paragraph 109
sections.original_text: 7.2	Stranded capacity protection for CN. If the La Crête facility is permanently destroyed, decommissioned, or otherwise ceases operations on a permanent basis during the Term, CN will be entitled to an early termination payment equal to the lesser of (a) the net present value of CN’s expected take-or-pay revenue for the remainder of the Term (discounted at the then-current long-term Government of Canada bond rate plus 200 basis points) and (b) the insurance proceeds available to Power Wood under paragraph 7.1 attributable to the CN take-or-pay obligation. Payment of the termination amount releases CN from all further service obligations under this Framework.
sections.revised_text: 7.2	Stranded capacity protection for CN. If the La Crête facility is permanently destroyed, decommissioned, or otherwise ceases operations on a permanent basis during the Term, CN will be entitled to an early termination payment equal to the lesser of (a) the net present value of CN’s expected take-or-pay revenue for the remainder of the Term (discounted at the then-current long-term Government of Canada bond rate plus 200 basis points) and (b) the insurance proceeds available to Power Wood under paragraph 7.1 attributable to the CN take-or-pay obligation. Payment of the termination amount releases each of CN and Power Wood from all further obligations under this Framework, other than obligations then accrued.

sections.label: Paragraph 111
sections.original_text: 7.3	Force Majeure. Neither party is in breach of this Framework during a period of Force Majeure (fire, flood, earthquake, extreme weather, pandemic, third-party labour disruption, regulatory action preventing operations, rail network disruption not attributable to the affected party’s own operations, or war or civil disturbance), provided the affected party gives prompt written notice and takes commercially reasonable steps to mitigate. Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation and CN’s service obligation are each suspended during Force Majeure. If Force Majeure extends beyond 180 consecutive days, either party may terminate this Framework on written notice without further liability.
sections.revised_text: 7.3	Force Majeure. Neither party is in breach of this Framework during a period of Force Majeure (fire, flood, earthquake, extreme weather, pandemic, third-party labour disruption, regulatory action preventing operations, rail network disruption not attributable to the affected party’s own operations, or war or civil disturbance), provided the affected party gives prompt written notice and takes commercially reasonable steps to mitigate. Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation and CN’s service obligation are each suspended during Force Majeure. If the Force Majeure event affects CN’s rail network or operations, CN shall, using commercially reasonable efforts, repair, rebuild, or otherwise restore the affected infrastructure and resume service under paragraph 3 as soon as reasonably practicable. Neither party may terminate this Framework by reason of Force Majeure.

sections.label: Paragraph 113
sections.original_text: 7.4	CN service failure. If CN fails to provide the service specified in paragraph 3 and the failure is not excused by Force Majeure, Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation is suspended for the duration of the failure and CN is responsible for direct and foreseeable damages Power Wood incurs with its off-take counterparties as a direct consequence, subject to a liability cap equal to [NTD: confirm — proposed 12 months of Freight Rate revenue].
sections.revised_text: 7.4	CN service failure. If CN fails to provide the service specified in paragraph 3 and the failure is not excused by Force Majeure, Power Wood’s take-or-pay obligation is suspended for the duration of the failure and CN is responsible for all direct and foreseeable damages Power Wood incurs with respect to the operation of the La Crête facility, the High Level transload, and the Traffic (including, without limitation, damages incurred with off-take counterparties) as a direct consequence of CN’s service failure, subject to a liability cap equal to [NTD: confirm — proposed 12 months of Freight Rate revenue] [NTD: consider whether liability cap should be removed entirely].

sections.label: Paragraph 116
sections.original_text: 8.	Term and Parallel Conditions Precedent
sections.revised_text: 8.	Term and Parallel Conditions Precedent

sections.label: Paragraph 118
sections.original_text: 8.1	Initial Term. The Service Period runs for an initial term of 10 years from the Commencement Date, with good-faith renewal discussions commencing not later than 24 months before expiry. [NTD: Confirm with lenders whether 10 years is sufficient for debt amortization, or whether a 12- to 15-year term is required.]
sections.revised_text: 8.1	Initial Term. The Service Period runs for an initial term of 10 years from the Commencement Date, with good-faith renewal discussions commencing not later than 24 months before expiry. [NTD: Confirm with lenders whether 10 years is sufficient for debt amortization, or whether a 12- to 15-year term is required.]

sections.label: Paragraph 120
sections.original_text: 8.2	Parallel conditionality. The commercial arrangement contemplated by this Framework is one of mutual dependence: Power Wood cannot perform without the service commitment from CN, and CN’s service commitment is not required to be performed until Power Wood is ready to tender Traffic. The Framework resolves that mutual dependence by synchronizing both parties’ obligations on the parallel conditions precedent in paragraphs 8.3 and 8.4. Each is objective and commercially defined; none of them confers on either party a discretion to reopen the commercial terms of this Framework at commencement.
sections.revised_text: 8.2	Parallel conditionality. The commercial arrangement contemplated by this Framework is one of mutual dependence: Power Wood cannot perform without the service commitment from CN, and CN’s service commitment is not required to be performed until Power Wood is ready to tender Traffic. The Framework resolves that mutual dependence by synchronizing both parties’ obligations on the parallel conditions precedent in paragraphs 8.3 and 8.4. Each is objective and commercially defined; none of them confers on either party a discretion to reopen the commercial terms of this Framework at commencement.

sections.label: Paragraph 122
sections.original_text: 8.3	Power Wood conditions precedent. Power Wood’s obligations under paragraphs 3 to 7 become binding upon satisfaction of each of:
sections.revised_text: 8.3	Power Wood conditions precedent. Power Wood’s obligations under paragraphs 3 to 7 become binding upon satisfaction of each of:

sections.label: Paragraph 124
sections.original_text: (a)	Financial Close of Power Wood’s construction financing, including delivery of the Lender Direct Agreement under paragraph 9;
sections.revised_text: (a)	Financial Close of Power Wood’s construction financing, including delivery of the Lender Direct Agreement under paragraph 9;

sections.label: Paragraph 126
sections.original_text: (b)	Construction Completion of the La Crête facility;
sections.revised_text: (b)	Construction Completion of the La Crête facility;

sections.label: Paragraph 128
sections.original_text: (c)	High Level Transload readiness, meaning the unit train loop, transload, and loading infrastructure at High Level is constructed in accordance with the CN Prior Positions and is accepted by CN under paragraph 8.4(a);
sections.revised_text: (c)	High Level Transload readiness, meaning the unit train loop, transload, and loading infrastructure at High Level is constructed in accordance with the CN Prior Positions and is accepted by CN under paragraph 8.4(a);

sections.label: Paragraph 130
sections.original_text: (d)	Port access, meaning Power Wood holds a confirmed terminal services arrangement at the Port of Prince Rupert sufficient for the Traffic at Phase 2 volumes [NTD: client to confirm current Port of Prince Rupert terminal access status]; and
sections.revised_text: (d)	Port access, meaning Power Wood holds a confirmed terminal services arrangement at the Port of Prince Rupert sufficient for the Traffic at Phase 2 volumes [NTD: client to confirm current Port of Prince Rupert terminal access status]; and

sections.label: Paragraph 132
sections.original_text: (e)	Regulatory approvals, meaning all required environmental, operational, and forestry permits for the La Crête facility are in good standing.
sections.revised_text: (e)	Regulatory approvals, meaning all required environmental, operational, and forestry permits for the La Crête facility are in good standing.

sections.label: Paragraph 134
sections.revised_text: (f)	Off-take agreements, meaning Power Wood has binding off-take agreements in place sufficient to support [X%] of the Phase 2 Volume Schedule.

sections.label: Paragraph 136
sections.original_text: [NTD: Confirm whether the 90-acre High Level site option has been exercised; if not, exercise is a further condition precedent under this paragraph.]
sections.revised_text: [NTD: Confirm whether the 90-acre High Level site option has been exercised; if not, exercise is a further condition precedent under this paragraph.]

sections.label: Paragraph 137
sections.revised_text: Power Wood may, in its sole discretion, waive any one or more of the conditions precedent in this paragraph 8.3 by written notice to CN.

sections.label: Paragraph 139
sections.original_text: 8.4	CN conditions precedent. CN’s obligations under paragraph 3 become binding simultaneously with the satisfaction of Power Wood’s conditions precedent, subject only to each of the following objective conditions, none of which may be exercised to revisit the commercial terms of this Framework:
sections.revised_text: 8.4	CN conditions precedent. CN’s obligations under paragraph 3 become binding simultaneously with the satisfaction of Power Wood’s conditions precedent, subject only to each of the following objective conditions, none of which may be exercised to revisit the commercial terms of this Framework:

sections.label: Paragraph 141
sections.original_text: (a)	Technical acceptance of the High Level transload against CN’s published technical and safety standards, with written identification of any deficiencies and an opportunity for Power Wood to cure. CN’s inspection right is a technical-compliance right only, not a commercial-renegotiation right.
sections.revised_text: (a)	Technical acceptance of the High Level transload against CN’s published technical and safety standards, with written identification of any deficiencies and an opportunity for Power Wood to cure. CN’s inspection right is a technical-compliance right only, not a commercial-renegotiation right.

sections.label: Paragraph 143
sections.original_text: (b)	Regulatory standing, meaning CN is not subject to any Agency order limiting or suspending operations on the MKNR.
sections.revised_text: (b)	Regulatory standing, meaning CN is not subject to any Agency order limiting or suspending operations on the MKNR, provided that this condition is not satisfied (and CN may not rely on this condition) if the order was made at the request of, or was otherwise initiated directly or indirectly by, CN.

sections.label: Paragraph 145
sections.original_text: (c)	Notice of commencement from Power Wood confirming that each of its conditions precedent has been satisfied.
sections.revised_text: (c)	Notice of commencement from Power Wood confirming that each of its conditions precedent has been satisfied.

sections.label: Paragraph 147
sections.original_text: 8.5	Rejection of open-ended reservation. For greater certainty, and notwithstanding any contrary CN statement, CN may not, upon satisfaction of the parallel conditions precedent, add new commercial conditions, reopen any commercial term of this Framework, or decline to perform on the basis of “circumstances then existing” or any similar reservation. CN’s obligations, once triggered, are fixed and binding on the commercial terms set out herein.
sections.revised_text: 8.5	Rejection of open-ended reservation. For greater certainty, and notwithstanding any contrary CN statement, CN may not, upon satisfaction of the parallel conditions precedent, add new commercial conditions, reopen any commercial term of this Framework, or decline to perform on the basis of “circumstances then existing” or any similar reservation. CN’s obligations, once triggered, are fixed and binding on the commercial terms set out herein.

sections.label: Paragraph 149
sections.original_text: 8.6	Longstop date. If the Commencement Date has not occurred by the date that is five years from the date on which this Framework is delivered to CN or is ordered by the Agency (whichever is earlier), either party may terminate this Framework on written notice, without further liability except for obligations then accrued.
sections.revised_text: 8.6	Longstop date. If the Commencement Date has not occurred by the date that is five years [NTD: confirm five-year period with the business] from the date on which this Framework is agreed by the parties or is ordered by the Agency (whichever is earlier), either party may terminate this Framework on written notice, without further liability except for obligations then accrued.

sections.label: Paragraph 152
sections.original_text: 9.	Lender Alignment
sections.revised_text: 9.	Lender Alignment

sections.label: Paragraph 154
sections.original_text: 9.1	Lender Direct Agreement. CN acknowledges that Power Wood’s construction and term financing requires the Framework to be enforceable by and assignable to Power Wood’s lenders on a project finance basis. Concurrently with Power Wood’s Financial Close, CN will enter into a tripartite direct agreement (the “Lender Direct Agreement”) with Power Wood and Power Wood’s lenders (or an administrative agent on their behalf), containing:
sections.revised_text: 9.1	Lender Direct Agreement. CN acknowledges that Power Wood’s construction and term financing requires the Framework to be enforceable by and assignable to Power Wood’s lenders on a project finance basis. Concurrently with Power Wood’s Financial Close, CN will enter into a tripartite direct agreement (the “Lender Direct Agreement”) with Power Wood and Power Wood’s lenders (or an administrative agent on their behalf), containing:

sections.label: Paragraph 156
sections.original_text: (a)	Step-in rights permitting the lenders, upon an event of default by Power Wood under the financing documents, to step into and continue Power Wood’s performance of this Framework directly or through a substitute operator reasonably acceptable to CN;
sections.revised_text: (a)	Step-in rights permitting the lenders, upon an event of default by Power Wood under the financing documents, to step into and continue Power Wood’s performance of this Framework directly or through a substitute operator reasonably acceptable to CN;

sections.label: Paragraph 158
sections.original_text: (b)	Cure rights permitting the lenders not less than 30 days’ notice and opportunity to cure any Power Wood default under this Framework before CN may exercise termination remedies;
sections.revised_text: (b)	Cure rights permitting the lenders not less than 30 days’ notice and opportunity to cure any Power Wood default under this Framework before CN may exercise termination remedies;

sections.label: Paragraph 160
sections.original_text: (c)	Assignment to substitute operator, permitting the lenders to assign Power Wood’s rights and obligations to a substitute operator without further CN consent, provided the substitute operator is financially and operationally capable of performance and has expressly assumed Power Wood’s obligations in writing;
sections.revised_text: (c)	Assignment to substitute operator, permitting the lenders to assign Power Wood’s rights and obligations to a substitute operator without further CN consent, provided the substitute operator is financially and operationally capable of performance and has expressly assumed Power Wood’s obligations in writing;

sections.label: Paragraph 162
sections.original_text: (d)	No termination for insolvency, precluding CN from terminating this Framework solely by reason of Power Wood’s insolvency, bankruptcy, or restructuring, provided the lenders or a substitute operator continue performance; and
sections.revised_text: (d)	No termination for insolvency, precluding CN from terminating this Framework solely by reason of Power Wood’s insolvency, bankruptcy, or restructuring, provided the lenders or a substitute operator continue performance; and

sections.label: Paragraph 164
sections.original_text: (e)	Information rights in respect of material notices under this Framework.
sections.revised_text: (e)	Information rights in respect of material notices under this Framework.

sections.label: Paragraph 166
sections.original_text: Delivery of the executed Lender Direct Agreement is a condition precedent to Financial Close and, accordingly, to the operative effect of Power Wood’s commitments under this Framework.
sections.revised_text: Delivery of the executed Lender Direct Agreement is a condition precedent to Financial Close and, accordingly, to the operative effect of Power Wood’s commitments under this Framework.

sections.label: Paragraph 168
sections.original_text: [NTD: Financing counsel to confirm the preferred form of Lender Direct Agreement once the lenders are identified.]
sections.revised_text: [NTD: Financing counsel to confirm the preferred form of Lender Direct Agreement once the lenders are identified.]

sections.label: Paragraph 170
sections.original_text: 9.2	Project finance standards. The terms of this Framework taken as a whole — the Initial Term, the take-or-pay structure and L/C security, the plant disruption insurance with CN as loss payee, the parallel conditions precedent, the longstop date, and the Lender Direct Agreement — reflect standard project finance requirements for rail-dependent infrastructure at Power Wood’s scale. Their presence is itself evidence that the arrangement is real, structured, and lender-underwritten.
sections.revised_text: 9.2	Project finance standards. The terms of this Framework taken as a whole — the Initial Term, the take-or-pay structure and L/C security, the plant disruption insurance with CN as loss payee, the parallel conditions precedent, the longstop date, and the Lender Direct Agreement — reflect standard project finance requirements for rail-dependent infrastructure at Power Wood’s scale. Their presence is itself evidence that the arrangement is real, structured, and lender-underwritten.

sections.label: Paragraph 173
sections.original_text: 10.	Governance and Dispute Resolution
sections.revised_text: 10.	Governance and Dispute Resolution

sections.label: Paragraph 175
sections.original_text: 10.1	Joint Operational Committee. Within 60 days of the trigger of the parallel conditions precedent, the parties will establish a Joint Operational Committee (the “JOC”) of two representatives per party, meeting not less than quarterly, with scope covering operational forecasting, car supply planning, KPI review, safety and emergency coordination, and first-tier resolution of operational disputes.
sections.revised_text: 10.1	Joint Operational Committee. Within 60 days of the trigger of the parallel conditions precedent, the parties will establish a Joint Operational Committee (the “JOC”) of two representatives per party, meeting not less than quarterly, with scope covering operational forecasting, car supply planning, KPI review, safety and emergency coordination, and first-tier resolution of operational disputes.

sections.label: Paragraph 177
sections.original_text: 10.2	Tiered dispute resolution. Any dispute arising under this Framework will be resolved through: (a) Tier 1 — referral to the JOC; (b) Tier 2 — escalation to senior executives of CN (not below Vice-President, Commercial) and Power Wood (not below Chief Executive Officer) for 30 days of good-faith discussion; and (c) Tier 3 — arbitration under s. 169.31 CTA or any other dispute mechanism available under the CTA at the relevant time. Neither party loses any rights under the CTA as a consequence of participating in this process.
sections.revised_text: 10.2	Tiered dispute resolution. Any dispute arising under this Framework will be resolved through: (a) Tier 1 — referral to the JOC; (b) Tier 2 — escalation to senior executives of CN (not below Vice-President, Commercial) and Power Wood (not below Chief Executive Officer) for 30 days of good-faith discussion; (c) Tier 3 — Expert determination. For any dispute that is technical, operational, or valuation-related in nature (including KPI measurement disputes, capital cost allocation disputes, and Freight Rate review disputes), either party may refer the matter to expert determination by an independent expert jointly appointed by the parties (or, failing agreement, appointed by [NTD: appointing authority to confirm]), whose determination shall be final and binding on the referred issue; and (d) Tier 4 — arbitration under s. 169.31 CTA or any other dispute mechanism available under the CTA at the relevant time. Neither party loses any rights under the CTA as a consequence of participating in this process.

sections.label: Paragraph 180
sections.original_text: 11.	CN Invited Response
sections.revised_text: 11.	Without Prejudice

sections.label: Paragraph 177
sections.original_text: 11.1	Power Wood invites CN to respond to this Framework on the basis of the following alternatives:

sections.label: Paragraph 179
sections.original_text: (a)	Acceptance in writing that this Framework (as filed or with agreed amendments) constitutes the basis of a binding service commitment, following which the parties will document it in a definitive service agreement on terms consistent with this Framework;

sections.label: Paragraph 181
sections.original_text: (b)	Specific amendments, identifying for each proposed amendment the provision, the proposed amended language, and CN’s commercial or operational reason; or

sections.label: Paragraph 183
sections.original_text: (c)	Specific objections, stated with particularity on a clause-by-clause basis together with the commercial or operational basis for each objection.

sections.label: Paragraph 185
sections.original_text: 11.2	Response window. Power Wood requests that CN respond within [NTD: confirm — proposed 30 days] of CN’s receipt of the finalized Framework.

sections.label: Paragraph 183
sections.original_text: 11.3	Without prejudice. This Framework is tendered without prejudice to either party’s rights in CTA Case No. 26-05910. CN’s engagement with it is not a waiver or admission, and Power Wood’s delivery of it is not a withdrawal of the relief sought in the Reply. What Power Wood cannot accept, consistent with the commercial realities of the project, is a further period of indefinite deferral.
sections.revised_text: 11.1	Without prejudice. This Framework is tendered without prejudice to either party’s rights in CTA Case No. 26-05910. CN’s engagement with it is not a waiver or admission, and Power Wood’s delivery of it is not a withdrawal of the relief sought in the Reply. What Power Wood cannot accept, consistent with the commercial realities of the project, is a further period of indefinite deferral.

sections.label: Paragraph 186
sections.original_text: 12.	Response to CN’s Answer
sections.revised_text: 12.	Response to CN’s Answer

sections.revised_text: ¶ 52 | The Evaluation Approach cannot apply absent actual traffic | Once the Framework is on the record as a s. 116(1.2)(d) undertaking, the Evaluation Approach is engaged: traffic, request, undertaking, and operational requirements are each specified
